w:r.w:t: Refined from C2A Transmutation Chain | v1
Don't make the underground machine solve everything — liberate and route material into easier downstream processes.

The Excavator Robot is a penetrate–process–lift machine that breaks and lifts ground material, uses a binary intake gate to split likely-usable soil from obvious reject, and sends the coarse-qualified stream to a surface gravity-sorting container for downstream fabrication.
The Excavator Robot is the machine that makes the site buildable and turns the ground into a usable feed stream.
Site prep — loosen / level / open the ground, support foundation area preparation, help create workable terrain
Earth harvesting — extract ground material, coarse-separate usable soil from obvious reject, send processable earth upward into a surface sorting container
A ground liberator + coarse classifier + feedstream supplier.
Think of drinking something mixed with chunks.
You try to suck rocks, roots, clumps, and powder through one straw
First you shred the soft chunks
Then you lift the mixture upward
Then a gate decides what is small/processable enough to pass
Above ground, a larger container finishes the separation in open space
Do not make one underground super-machine
Do not demand clean ground categories from raw earth
Lift material upward instead of sorting deeply underground
Use a simple binary gate for coarse separation
Send likely usable soil to a surface container
Let the surface container finish secondary separation
Let downstream systems decide fine material quality
Raw ground comes out mixed: dirt, rocks, roots, clumps, debris, wet and dry mess. The ground does not come out in clean categories.

Make the excavator work like the survey bot in a sense: it has a pole that goes down, blades break up what is inside, air lifts the material upward, and a filter can help decide what is needed from the ground.
Suction alone fails on wet clumps, roots, sticky material, and mixed debris.
The blades within the pole should completely decimate whatever is within the pole so everything can be lifted more easily.
Intake zone normalized before suction tries to move material
If the shredder tries to destroy rocks too, it becomes fragile or expensive.
Transmutation: Soft-Material Processor, Not Rock Crusher
The blades should shred roots and clumps but not do anything to stones. Their shape can be curved so rocks are not broken by them, but instead get caught/deflected and left or rejected.
Curved blade geometry deflects hard objects rather than engaging them
Narrows purpose and reduces cost and wear
Rejected hard objects can still clog the intake area.
After the needed material is lifted, the leftover debris and hard objects can also be brought up and pushed out through a different exit.
Even with dual streams, reject and usable material can become a messy surface pile.
Transmutation: Lift First, Sort Above Ground
Remove the complicated internal filter and bring everything to the surface, then filter there where it is simpler.
Core move: lift first, sort above ground
Complexity moved from the hardest place (underground) to the easiest place (surface)
If lifted material just explodes onto the surface randomly, the sorting stage becomes messy and unreliable.
Transmutation: Flex Tube to Controlled Surface Delivery
Use a vacuum-like stretchy tube that carries the material to containers on the surface. The container handles the filtering and can connect directly to Fabricator.
System becomes: excavation head → flex tube → surface sorting container
A surface container can still jam if everything enters as one mixed mess.
Transmutation: Coarse Split First, Gravity Finish Second
Make the container vertical so gravity helps move things downward through chambers. Then you noticed the deeper truth: good soil should be separated from the rest before it reaches the container.
Vertical multi-chamber container uses gravity assist
Coarse gate splits stream before surface container receives it
Good soil and reject arrive at separate destinations
The first-pass separator must be simple and binary, not smart and fragile.
Transmutation: Binary Gate: Pass or Fail

The blades cut everything within the pole, then the air lifts it, and the filter is the decider: if it passes the filter, it goes into the good-soil container; if it cannot pass, it goes to the other container.
Gate is strictly binary: pass = likely usable, fail = reject
No intelligence required at the gate itself — size is the only criterion
Even if material is the right size, it may still be the wrong quality.
Transmutation: Hand Off to Downstream, Don't Solve It Here
The container or downstream process handles that through its own process if there is something in that regard.
Excavator does not solve final material-quality truth
Hands off size-qualified stream to: surface container, Fabricator, Verification, soil intelligence downstream
Five gaps identified after blueprint review. Each adds predictive capability, safety logic, wear awareness, or a cross-system data path the original blueprint left open.

The blueprint lists Survey as an input but treats it as a zone map only. The Survey Robot's penetration resistance curve already contains depth-specific data — where the soft layer ends, where resistance spikes, and how thick the workable material is. The Excavator currently discovers this blind, at the moment the blade load changes.

Before engaging any zone, the Excavator loads the Survey's penetration curve for that zone. This pre-arms the robot with expected blade load thresholds by depth, expected hard-layer onset depth, and a predicted reject ratio. The robot plans engagement depth and lift strategy before the pole enters the ground rather than reacting after it already has. New field: expected_hard_layer_depth_m loaded from Survey before penetration begins.
Survey penetration curve consumed as pre-mission depth profile per zone
Blade load targets and hard-layer onset depth pre-loaded before engagement
Predicted reject ratio available before extraction begins
New input field: expected_hard_layer_depth_m from Survey data
Excavator stops discovering the subsurface blind

The blueprint handles wet clumps as a shredding challenge but does not address what happens when ground moisture is high enough that the lifted stream becomes slurry. Slurry coats the gate, bridges the hose, and floods the surface container rather than separating cleanly. The Survey Robot already measures moisture per point — that data is unused by the Excavator.

Before extraction, the Excavator reads the zone moisture score from Survey data. Above a defined moisture threshold, lift speed is reduced and the binary gate aperture is adjusted to account for clump cohesion. If moisture is high enough to risk slurry, the system flags to House Brain before committing to extraction — rather than discovering the problem mid-flow with a clogged hose.
Pre-extraction moisture check from Survey zone record
Lift speed reduced above moisture threshold to prevent slurry formation
Gate aperture adjustment for high-cohesion conditions
Pre-extraction slurry-risk flag to House Brain if moisture exceeds safe extraction limit
New input field: zone_moisture_pct consumed from Survey before lift begins
Refinement 3 — The Blade Wear Blindness Problem

The blueprint uses blade load estimate only for jam/clog detection at runtime. Blade geometry — specifically the curved deflection profile — is what makes rock avoidance work. As blades wear, that geometry degrades and a blade designed to deflect rocks begins catching them. The system has no mechanism to detect this drift before a field failure occurs.

Blade load patterns over time are tracked as a wear proxy. The ratio of high-load events to cumulative throughput volume is computed continuously. As that ratio drifts upward over time, it indicates blade wear rather than just a hard-ground episode. The system emits a blade wear index with each excavation record and flags inspection before the deflection geometry is gone. New field: blade_wear_index emitted per excavation record.
Cumulative blade load-per-throughput ratio tracked as wear proxy
Blade wear index computed and emitted with every excavation record
Wear threshold triggers pre-emptive blade inspection flag to House Brain
Failure mode shifted from reactive (blade fails in field) to predictive (flag before geometry degradation)
New emitted field: blade_wear_index per excavation record
Refinement 4 — The Surface Container Blind Spot

The Excavator routes to container_id and assumes reception. No confirmation handshake exists. If the container is full, jammed, or disconnected, the Excavator keeps pumping material, causing hose backpressure, bridging, and potential system jam. The blueprint has no mechanism to detect or respond to this before it becomes a failure.

Before starting lift operations, the Excavator sends a container-ready confirmation request. The surface container responds with its current fill level and pressure state. During operation, fill level and backpressure are monitored continuously. If fill exceeds threshold or backpressure rises, the Excavator pauses lift and waits rather than forcing material through. New command: confirm_container_ready(container_id) added before start_lift(). New live inputs: container_fill_pct and backpressure_state.
Pre-lift container-ready handshake required before extraction begins
Live fill level and backpressure monitored during operation
Lift automatically paused when container fill or backpressure exceeds threshold
New operational inputs: container_fill_pct and backpressure_state
Cross-System Refinement — Excavator Feeds Survey Calibration — Cross-System Data Path

The Survey Robot classifies zones from surface probing. Once the Excavator actually digs, it gets ground-truth subsurface data the Survey never had: actual blade load at depth, actual reject ratio, actual hard-layer encounter depth, actual material split. This is the most direct real-world confirmation or contradiction of Survey predictions. Currently that data path does not exist in either robot's architecture.

After completing each zone, the Excavator emits a post-excavation ground-truth record to the Survey library and House Brain. This record compares actual hard layer depth vs Survey prediction, actual reject ratio vs expected, and actual blade load profile. Over many zones and builds, this calibrates Survey's penetration-to-classification model — making future surveys more accurate at no additional probing cost. Survey predicted BUILDABLE but Excavator found 40% reject at 0.8m? That is a model correction that improves every future survey on similar terrain.
New output channel: excavator_ground_truth_record emitted to Survey library after each zone
Record includes: actual vs predicted hard layer depth, actual vs predicted reject ratio, blade load profile by depth
Survey classification model updated from Excavator field results over time
Prediction accuracy improves across builds without additional Survey passes
Survey blueprint updated: new input source from Excavator post-zone ground truth
Load Survey penetration curve and moisture data before engaging each zone
Confirm surface container readiness before starting lift
Locally break and shred soft earth, clumps, and roots
Lift a mixed material stream upward
Use a coarse intake gate to split likely-usable soil from obvious reject
Send likely-usable stream through tube to the surface container
Send reject material to the reject path
Support site prep by clearing and opening workable ground
Track blade wear index and flag inspection threshold
Emit excavation and material-feed telemetry to House Brain
Emit post-zone ground-truth record to Survey library
A coarse-qualified material stream for downstream fabrication
It is the front-end physical interface between land and fabrication feedstock.
Expected hard layer depth per zone
container_fill_pct — live fill level during operation
backpressure_state — live pressure signal during lift
Requested feed class / desired particle band
Whether extraction is for fabrication feed, fill, road base, or general clearing
Blade wear index and inspection flags
Slurry-risk flag when moisture threshold exceeded
Actual hard layer depth vs predicted
Central vertical excavation pole / intake head
Internal shredding blades with curved deflection geometry
This is not a classic big bucket excavator. It is a penetrate–process–lift machine.
Deflect hard objects without engaging them
Make below-ground behavior simple and predictable
Purpose: shred roots, clumps, compacted soft earth, fibrous debris.
Blades are not rock crushers. Curved geometry processes soft material and deflects hard objects. This is what keeps wear and cost under control — and what degrades first as blades wear.
Purpose: carry mixed material from below ground to the surface in a controlled path.
Passes gate → goes to good-soil/surface container. Fails gate → goes to reject stream.
The gate is not trying to know chemistry or final quality. Only: processable enough or not.
Receives the likely-usable stream and performs secondary separation in open space.
Emits fill level and backpressure state to Excavator during operation
Blade load estimate (also used for wear index computation)
Optional camera for gross context only
Container fill pct and backpressure state
Survey data loader — penetration curve and moisture per zone

Load Survey penetration curve and zone moisture score. Pre-compute expected hard layer depth, expected reject ratio, and slurry risk. Flag to House Brain if moisture exceeds extraction threshold before any movement begins.
Send confirm_container_ready(container_id). Proceed only when container_fill_pct and backpressure_state are within acceptable bounds. Do not begin lift until confirmed.
Move to excavation/prep zone and penetrate to target depth. Blade load targets loaded from Survey prediction.
Internal blades shred soft clumps, roots, and messy organic material. Hard objects deflected by curved blade geometry. Blade wear index updated continuously.
Move loosened mixed stream upward through controlled lift path. Monitor backpressure. Pause lift if container fill threshold exceeded.
At the intake gate: likely usable material passes, obvious reject does not. Gate aperture adjusted for high-moisture conditions if flagged.
Good-size stream goes through hose/tube to vertical surface container. Reject stream goes to reject path/container.

After zone completion, emit excavator_ground_truth_record to Survey library: actual hard layer depth vs predicted, actual reject ratio vs predicted, blade load profile by depth. Report excavation telemetry to House Brain.
13.3 Ground Truth Record — Emitted to Survey Library
Blade wear threshold exceeded — deflection geometry degraded
Too much reject entering good stream
Too much good material lost to reject
Slurry formation when moisture threshold not caught pre-extraction
Container not ready — no pre-lift confirmation obtained
Container fills faster than extraction pauses
Backpressure spike not detected — hose bridge occurs
Stale or missing Survey penetration curve for zone
Excavation in marginal/reject area without override
Ground truth record not emitted — Survey calibration gap
Excavator is successful if it can:
Prepare the site physically enough for downstream work
Extract ground material without becoming a giant mining machine
Shred soft messy material without trying to crush stones
Pre-detect slurry risk from Survey moisture data before extraction begins
Confirm container readiness before lift and pause lift when container is full
Produce a steady likely-usable soil stream
Track blade wear and flag inspection before deflection geometry is gone
Hand off the stream to the surface container reliably
Emit ground-truth records that calibrate Survey classification accuracy over time
Survey already qualified the work zone and provides penetration curve and moisture data
Target depth and extraction goal are provided by House Brain
A surface sorting container exists and can emit fill/pressure state
Reject material has a routing destination
Ground is not solid bedrock beyond its intended operating envelope
Survey library has an input channel to receive Excavator ground-truth records
Goal: prove break–lift–gate works on mixed test media.
Goal: prove material can be delivered cleanly upward.
Phase 3 — Reject Routing + Blade Wear Tracking
Goal: prove stones/roots do not kill throughput and wear is detectable early.
Goal: prove Excavator never over-fills or back-pressures the surface container.
Goal: Excavator enters every zone with a pre-known depth profile, not a blank slate.
Phase 6 — Ground Truth Emit + House Brain Integration
Goal: make Excavator a real part of the system feedback loop, not just a digger.
Not trying to understand every material chemically
Offloading fine sorting upward and outward
Using Survey data rather than onboard soil intelligence
Don't make the underground machine solve everything — liberate and route material into easier downstream processes, then feed what you learned back into the system.
19. Out of Scope / Not This Robot's Job
Those belong to: Survey, Fabricator, Verification, House Brain, and Assembly.
Excavator only says: "I can turn the ground into a coarse-qualified feed stream and a workable site."

The Excavator Robot is a penetrate–process–lift machine that pre-loads Survey depth and moisture profiles before engaging each zone, breaks and lifts ground material through a binary intake gate, pauses lift against a container-ready handshake, tracks blade wear as a predictive indicator, and emits post-zone ground-truth records that calibrate Survey classification accuracy across all future builds.
